Today I'm going to explain everything about the controversy surrounding the various versions of the Vivobook 15 equipped with Ryzen 7. And as a bonus, I'll show you in practice whether or not there are real differences between these two variants, since we bought, tested, and disassembled both to provide you with the best possible information.  So go ahead and leave a like, subscribe to the channel, and help us reach our goal of 100,000 subscribers.  So now let's get to the video.  If you're thinking about buying the Vivobook 15 equipped with Ryzen 7, you've probably already come across a controversy that's been going on, since Asus previously sold three variants, each with different features.  So the first variant is one that we've already shown here on the channel that came with a Ryzen 7000 series processor and had a TN panel.  And in that video we showed that it was a very good notebook, but that the screen was a bit of a hindrance.  In other words, it's a decent panel for light work, maybe for consuming content occasionally, but it's not an IPS panel. So the first thing you can notice is this, look, the angle of vision is completely impaired. So if you move the screen up, down, or even from side to side, you'll see that the image becomes completely distorted.  And then after that, Asus released some variations like the NJ 611, which came with an IPS screen, had the Ryzen 7 5825U processor, and offered almost unbeatable value for money, since the notebook had a good build quality and also came with 8 GB of RAM soldered onto the motherboard and an extra slot for upgrading.  In other words, upgrading was cheaper because you didn't need to remove the RAM stick and you could also activate dual channel.  Finally, there was the BQ 966 variant, which did not have memory soldered onto the board and only had one slot.  This means that in order to upgrade, you had to remove the RAM stick and install a larger one.  And with that, you would n't be able to activate dual channel, plus it would be much more expensive to upgrade, since you would have to remove one RAM stick and install a larger one. And what ended up happening was precisely the search for the NJ611 version, since it was by far the best value for money and, consequently, ended up being the best-selling.  However, in recent months Asus released an update that ended up leaving people very unhappy, since the NJ611 version is now basically the same as the BQ966 version, meaning it no longer has soldered memory on the board and only has one slot.  That's precisely why I bought these two NJ611 versions, and I'm going to show you the difference between them now.  In terms of construction, we have exactly the same notebook. I'll even put it here on the label, just so you can see that it's exactly the same model.  So, here it says M1502YA NJ611 and a little further down you 'll see the manufacturing date, 2026-02. So, this is still the older version that has 8 GB soldered on and an extra slot for upgrading.  In this other version, you'll see that it also says the same thing, M1502YA NJ611, but the manufacturing date is 2026-05. And in this new version, we don't have soldered memory.  And to discover the real difference between the machines, the first thing we did was take them apart.  And the first difference we could see in this new version was precisely here in the RAM memory slot , since in the previous version, when you bought the 8 GB version, the slot came empty and had 8 GB soldered onto the board.  Even in this version, you can see that the RAM slot is already occupied. Another difference we noticed was that they are now using Adata brand NVMe SSDs, whereas in the previous version they were using a different NVMe SSD.  Otherwise, the design is basically identical, featuring the same PCB construction and the same heat dissipation system.  Now take a look at this image of the two versions side by side so you can see how similar they really are, and what will change is precisely everything I've told you.  But the difference isn't just in the internal components of the two variants, as performance is directly affected by the inability to use Dual Channel.  This means that this new version will have inferior performance compared to the other version, which had 8 GB of soldered memory plus an extra slot for upgrades.  And to show you this difference, we ran several benchmark tests.  The first was the Performance Test, and this new version delivered 20,600 points in the CPU test and 1,800 points in the integrated graphics card test .  If we compare it to the older version, it delivered 20,500 points in the processor, which is a very similar performance, but in the integrated GPU it managed to reach 2,500 points, giving a difference of approximately 20 to 25%.  In Geekbench 6, it delivered 1,900 points in single-core performance, 6,400 points in multi-core performance, and approximately 14,000 points in GPU performance.  And if we compare it again with the old version, it manages to deliver 1,900 points in single core, 7,100 points in multi-core, and around 16,500 points in the integrated GPU test .
